title: Writing - Part 1: Artificial Intelligence in Society
description: Your class has attended a panel discussion on the impact of artificial intelligence on the job market and everyday life.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the points in your notes. You should explain which point raises the most serious concern for society, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Millions of low-skilled jobs will simply disappear within a decade.
AI systems collect enormous amounts of personal data without users realising.
Machines cannot be held accountable when an automated decision causes harm.

structure.introduction: Introduce the rapid rise of AI and the debate surrounding its societal impact.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Examine the threat AI poses to employment and the livelihoods of workers.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Discuss the ethical dilemmas created by automated decision-making.
structure.conclusion: Evaluate both concerns and argue which presents the greater challenge for society.

content.modelAnswer:
Few technological advances in recent history have provoked as much debate as artificial intelligence. Its rapid integration into commercial and public life has brought undeniable convenience, yet it has simultaneously given rise to profound concerns about the future of work and human accountability. This essay will examine the threat AI poses to employment and the ethical dilemmas inherent in automated decision-making.

The displacement of workers by automated systems is perhaps the most immediate concern. Routine and manual roles in industries such as manufacturing, logistics, and customer service are already being replaced by algorithms at an accelerating pace. While new types of employment may eventually emerge, the transition is likely to be painful for those whose skills become obsolete overnight. Without significant investment in retraining programmes, entire communities could face long-term unemployment and the social instability that accompanies it.

However, the ethical implications of AI decision-making arguably present an even deeper challenge. When an algorithm determines who receives a loan, a job interview, or a prison sentence, questions of transparency and accountability become critical. These systems can perpetuate and even amplify existing biases if trained on historically skewed data, yet their internal logic is often opaque. Unlike a human decision-maker, a machine cannot be questioned or held morally responsible for the harm it causes.

In conclusion, while job displacement is a serious and tangible threat, I believe the ethical dimension is the more pressing societal concern. Economic disruption can be mitigated through policy; ensuring that AI systems are fair, transparent, and accountable requires us to fundamentally rethink how we assign responsibility in an automated world.
